pages.textPlain: ONE THEORY OF HOW RALEIGH HILLS GOT ITS NAME Raleigh S. Robinson, who is a native of Washington county, now owns and operates a portion of the farm where he was born on the 2nd of May, 1857. His parents were William J. and Emeline (Barnum) Robinson, the father's birth having occurred in Mercer county, Pennsylvania, on the 12th of October, 1827, and that of the mother in Jefferson county, Indiana, on September 16, 1829.They were married in the latter county on the 9th of January, 1848, but began their domestic life in Jennings county, Indiana, where they resided until April, 1851. The children of pioneers, naturally they felt that better opportunities were afforded all young people in a new country, so despite their very limited means and the many hardships and dangers they knew lay before them, they courageously started across the plains to Oregon.The journey was long and tiresome, occupying nearly six months, but in September they arrived in Clackamas county, where they spent six weeks. In the following March Mr. Robinson filed on a donation claim in Washington, county, to which they removed and there spent the remainder of their lives. Raleigh Hills seems to be named after Raleigh Robinson. A post office named Raleigh was established in the area April 1892, and was closed twelve years later. Southern Pacific's Red Electric line had a stop in Raleigh from 1914 until the line ceased operation in 1929. A Raleigh Hills branch of the Portland post office was opened in 1968. Raleigh Hills is a neighborhood within the metropolitan area of Portland. It is located in the southwest hills in Washington County, bordered by Beaverton to the west, West Slope to the north, with Progress and Garden Home to the south. the population is about 6,000. The central area is located at the intersection of Oregon Routes 10 and 210. blocks.text: ONE THEORY OF HOW RALEIGH HILLS GOT ITS NAME

Raleigh S. Robinson, who is a native of Washington county, now owns and operates a portion of the farm where he was born on the 2nd of May, 1857. His parents were William J. and Emeline (Barnum) Robinson, the father's birth having occurred in Mercer county, Pennsylvania, on the 12th of October, 1827, and that of the mother in Jefferson county, Indiana, on September 16, 1829.They were married in the latter county on the 9th of January, 1848, but began their domestic life in Jennings county, Indiana, where they resided until April, 1851. The children of pioneers, naturally they felt that better opportunities were afforded all young people in a new country, so despite their very limited means and the many hardships and dangers they knew lay before them, they courageously started across the plains to Oregon.The journey was long and tiresome, occupying nearly six months, but in September they arrived in Clackamas county, where they spent six weeks. In the following March Mr. Robinson filed on a donation claim in Washington, county, to which they removed and there spent the remainder of their lives.

Raleigh Hills seems to be named after Raleigh Robinson. A post office named Raleigh was established in the area April 1892, and was closed twelve years later. Southern Pacific's Red Electric line had a stop in Raleigh from 1914 until the line ceased operation in 1929. A Raleigh Hills branch of the Portland post office was opened in 1968.

Raleigh Hills is a neighborhood within the metropolitan area of Portland. It is located in the southwest hills in Washington County, bordered by Beaverton to the west, West Slope to the north, with Progress and Garden Home to the south. the population is about 6,000.
The central area is located at the intersection of Oregon Routes 10 and 210. That is the Beaverton Hillsdale Highway and Scholls Ferry Road intersection:

pages.textPlain: When you help with school supplies it provides students with the tools they need to learn, boosts their confidence, and fosters a sense of belonging, ultimately contributing to their academic success and future opportunities.Providing school supplies ensures that all students, regardless of their socioeconomic background, have the necessary resources to participate fully in their education.Improved Academic Performance:Studies show that students with adequate supplies are more engaged, focused, and confident, leading to better attendance, higher grades, and increased participation in class.Enhanced Self-Esteem:When students have the supplies they need, they feel prepared and valued, which can boost their self-esteem and motivation to learn.Reduced Financial Burden on Families:Back-to-school shopping can be expensive, and helping with supplies can alleviate the financial strain on families, allowing them to focus on other essential needs.Positive Impact on Learning Environment:When students have the resources they need, it creates a more positive and supportive learning environment for everyone in the classroom. Raleigh Hills Business Association Mission: To support the community of Raleigh Hills; to promote a vibrant business environment, maintain a safe and healthy climate, create community consciousness and maximize livability. 4th Annual Shred/Recycle Event sponsored by RHBA Saturday, Apr 22, 9:00 AM – Noon 8555 SW Apple Way (in the AAA Oregon parking lot faces Beaverton Hillsdale Hwy), Portland, OR 97225 As a community service RHBA is paying the $450 to rent the shred truck from AccuNW. Bring up to 4 boxes of documents to shred (be mindful that we are not set up to take large office clean outs of documents). Mark your calendars we will be updating this post with those vendors who will be accepting goods that day. All these business accept your donations at any time not just during this RHBA Event, feel free to contact any of these businesses and donate today! We will also be accepting the following: – EcoBinary e recyclers www.ecobinary.com will be accepting any electronics including old cell phones, computers, monitors, office furnishings (except big screen TV’s), dead or alive – Cartridge Network www.cartridge-network.com will be accepting any sorts of toner cartridges – Interstate All Battery http://www.interstatebatteries.com will accepting batteries – Community Warehouse www.communitywarehouse “accepting” good useable household items that will help families get set up in households (kitchen utensils, bedding including mattress pads, blankets, sheets and pillows, towels, etc; single/double/queen only mattresses are accepted without stains, tears or rips. NO large furniture; no king size mattress, no large bed frames, no armoires or china hutches, no sofa beds or heavy recliners. – Garden Home Community Library will take books and movies. Washington County Sherriff Community Outreach will be present at the event. ITEMS WE WILL NOT BE TAKING!!! No Paint No Light Bulbs No Florescent Lights No Styrofoam No Big Screen TV’s No King size mattresses No Stained or Soiled Mattresses of any kind No Wardrobe Closets No Armories No Large Bulky Furniture No Plastic bags No Clothing No moving boxes or packing material Please note the items we’re taking will be re-purposed in some way avoiding the landfill. If you need more information contact us at: info@raleighhillsbusinessassn.org or visit our website raleighhillsbusinessassn.org

pages.textPlain: 2015 SHRED EVENT In the United States over 40,000 tons of plastic and metal is saved from landfills annually as a result of cartridge recycling. For every 100,000 used cartridges recycled, we can save 1,000,000liters of oil. Ink cartridge recycling has virtually become synonymous with successful sustainable development. Griffin Hampson from Cartridge Network processed over 150 cartridges that folks brought in to the SHRED EVENT and is quite pleased with the turnout. E-waste is the most rapidly growing segment of the municipal solid waste stream. Moreover this waste contains valuable materials such as aluminum, copper, gold and silver. In order to conserve natural resources and the energy needed to produce new electronic equipment from virgin resources, electronic equipment can be refurbished, reused, and recycled instead of being landfilled. What is called “E-waste” also contains toxic and hazardous materials including mercury and lead which have the potential to leach into our soil and water. Frankie Bax reported that Eco Binary received over 1300 pounds of electronics. At some point every business has to dispose of confidential information. Customer lists, confidential client information, credit card and sales receipts, personnel and payroll records all need to be kept secure. Both your customers and employees have the legal right to have this information protected. This provides some security for identity theft. Our paper shreader processed over 2 and a half tons of paper. Last year we were just a few pounds over 2 tons so that is a significant improvement. The truck will shred 9 tons so we have a way to go for next year. Batteries are made from a number of different materials including acid, lead, nickel, lithium, cadmium, alkaline and nickel metal hydride. Tossing your batteries in the trash sends them to an incinerator or landfill where their casing can disintegrate, releasing this toxic mix into our environment. Annette Fernald of Interstate All Battery collected 760 lbs of batteries of all sizes and shapes and has disposed of them in a safe manner The 10 Safe Computing Tips Are Patch, Patch, PATCH! Set up your computer for auto patching. An un-patched machine is more likely to have software vulnerabilities that can be exploited. Install protective software. Software is available as a free download for Windows, Macintosh, and Linux. Commercial software from Naorton McAfee and others. Choose strong passwords. Use letters, numbers, and special characters to create a mental image or an acronym that is easy for you to remember. Create a different password for each important account, and change passwords regularly. Backup, Backup, BACKUP! Backing up your machine regularly can protect you from the unexpected. Control access to your machine. Don’t leave your computer in an unsecured area, or unattended and logged on, especially in public places. Use email and the Internet safely. Ignore unsolicited emails, and be wary of attachments, links and forms in emails that come from people you don’t know. Use secure connections. When connected to the Internet, your data can be vulnerable while in transit. Protect sensitive data. Reduce the risk of identity theft. Securely remove sensitive data files from your hard drive, which is also recommended when recycling or repurposing your computer. Use desktop firewalls. Macintosh and Windows computers have basic desktop firewalls as part of their operating systems. When set up properly, these firewalls protect your computer files from being scanned. Most importantly, stay informed. Stay current with the latest developments for Windows, Macintosh Linux, and Unix systems. Stay Safe and watch for other items of interest as we discuss Emergency Preparedness for 2015 Neighborhood Night Out Tuesday August 5 Was a fun for all night getting acquainted with our neighbors and with the community services team from Washington County. A big thanks to Lee Blevins of the Washington County Sheriff’s Office for priming the pump for this project. Pictured below representing RHBA are Liz Utter – New Seasons Market, Robin Curtin – Univera and Diane Snedecor – Mountain Man Nut and Fruit Co. Thank you ladies for your dedication and thank you to all who turned out. June 11, 2014 RHBA Annual Meeting Annual Report April 26, 2014 Shred Event Raleigh Hills Our first Shred Event was a huge success. We send a bigh thank you to AAA Autosource for the use of their parking area on Saturday for the event. The cars were lined up even before the truck arrived for the event and we were taking boxes and sacks from folks to dump just as soon as they arrived. A special thank you to Diane Snedicor of Mountan Man Nut and Fruit as well as Mary Sherman of Sherman Properties for their sponsorship in the event. The rain was held at bay while we toiled away at shredding over 4000 pounds of unwanted paper items form more people than we could count. We all get a ton of junk in the mail, credit card applications, insurance packets, a retirement fund report from 10 years ago and more. Thankfully, deciding what to keep is simple, and once you do you can shred pretty much everything else. In general, you want to keep physical copies of anything related to state or federal matters, such as certifications, licenses, or deeds. The reason is twofold: you want to have easy access to these in case you need them, and they’re also a pain to replace because you typically need to make a direct request to the government agency, which takes a lot of time. Everything else you have you can safely shred. You should shred anything that has personal information like your name, address, phone number, social security number, or bank account information. This might include a few documents you don’t initially think about, including ATM receipts, credit card receipts, bills, and even used airline tickets. You should also immediately shred expired credit cards, visas, passports, and IDs.

Backup, Backup, BACKUP! Backing up your machine regularly can protect you from the unexpected.
Control access to your machine. Don’t leave your computer in an unsecured area, or unattended and logged on, especially in public places.
Use email and the Internet safely. Ignore unsolicited emails, and be wary of attachments, links and forms in emails that come from people you don’t know.
Use secure connections. When connected to the Internet, your data can be vulnerable while in transit.
Protect sensitive data. Reduce the risk of identity theft. Securely remove sensitive data files from your hard drive, which is also recommended when recycling or repurposing your computer.

Use desktop firewalls. Macintosh and Windows computers have basic desktop firewalls as part of their operating systems. When set up properly, these firewalls protect your computer files from being scanned.
Most importantly, stay informed. Stay current with the latest developments for Windows, Macintosh Linux, and Unix systems.

blocks.text: Stay Safe and watch for other items of interest as we discuss Emergency Preparedness for 2015

Tuesday August 5 Was a fun for all night getting acquainted with our neighbors and with the community services team from Washington County. A big thanks to Lee Blevins of the Washington County Sheriff’s Office for priming the pump for this project. Pictured below representing RHBA are Liz Utter – New Seasons Market, Robin Curtin – Univera and Diane Snedecor – Mountain Man Nut and Fruit Co. Thank you ladies for your dedication and thank you to all who turned out.

Our first Shred Event was a huge success. We send a bigh thank you to AAA Autosource for the use of their parking area on Saturday for the event. The cars were lined up even before the truck arrived for the event and we were taking boxes and sacks from folks to dump just as soon as they arrived. A special thank you to Diane Snedicor of Mountan Man Nut and Fruit as well as Mary Sherman of Sherman Properties for their sponsorship in the event. The rain was held at bay while we toiled away at shredding over 4000 pounds of unwanted paper items form more people than we could count. We all get a ton of junk in the mail, credit card applications, insurance packets, a retirement fund report from 10 years ago and more. Thankfully, deciding what to keep is simple, and once you do you can shred pretty much everything else. In general, you want to keep physical copies of anything related to state or federal matters, such as certifications, licenses, or deeds. The reason is twofold: you want to have easy access to these in case you need them, and they’re also a pain to replace because you typically need to make a direct request to the government agency, which takes a lot of time. Everything else you have you can safely shred. You should shred anything that has personal information like your name, address, phone number, social security number, or bank account information. This might include a few documents you don’t initially think about, including ATM receipts, credit card receipts, bills, and even used airline tickets. You should also immediately shred expired credit cards, visas, passports, and IDs.

pages.textPlain: Help Support Our Mural Mural Activity/Coloring Book $9.99/each (shipping charges additional TBA) To get your books please contact our office: info@RaleighHillsBusinessAssn.org The Stairway mural photo-shopped to get a full visual effect. About the Mural: It started as a conversation with two businesses in Raleigh Hills who were concerned about tagging and vandalism. As the managers of Raleigh Hills Fred Meyer and Courtyard Village researched anti-graffiti protective covering, an idea for producing a public art project was born. Thanks to generous funding from Venture Portland, Explore Tualatin Valley and support from the Raleigh Hills Business Association, the Mural on The Stairs project began. The active senior living community at Courtyard Village helped shape this project by providing invaluable feedback. They participated in surveys and discussions with our local historian Carolyn Woods. We felt that their inclusion and the connection and engagement with them emulated the tradition of respect for elders in the native culture. Our muralist, Stacy Lynn Smith, worked to tie in all the elements that were important to celebrate and appreciate here and now in Raleigh Hills. The Gorge was the lifeline for commerce, hunting, fishing and transportation. The outside wall pays homage to the Columbia Gorge. Special thanks to Bob Hansen for the beautiful text work that he contributed. The inside walls of the stairs celebrate the salmon, the wildlife and native plants that existed when the “first peoples” walked here, lived here and had their families here. It is their footsteps we honor. When people walk up or down the stairs they are embraced by the peace of the landscape as it was in those days To view the mural, please park in the RH Fred Meyer parking lot, off SW 77th Avenue. There is no public parking at Courtyard Village. Info contact RHBA: info@RaleighHillsBusinessAssn.org https://raleighhillsbusinessassn.org/mural ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ The RHBA Mural Team Collaborated with the Muralists: Stacy Lynn Smith https://www.stacylynnsmith.com/ and Bob Hansen https://www.inknife.co/ RHBA received 2 Grants; 1 from Venture Portland https://ventureportland.org/ and the 2nd from Explore Tualatin Valley https://tualatinvalley.org/. The importance of the Camas Plants to our local History. The Kalapoya natives came to Beaverton, OR area to harvest camas plants, which were a vital food source for them. They practice sustainable harvesting techniques, ensuring the plants thrived for future generations, and camas played a significant role in their cultural practices and seasonal ceremonies. Kalapoya Natives and the Beaverton Area Historical Presence The Kalapoya people originally occupied a vast area in the Willamette Valley. Including regions that encompass modern-day Beaverton, OR. They have lived in this area for over 14,000 years, developing a deep connection to the land and its resources. Importance of Camas Plants Camas plants were a vital food source for the Kalapoya. The bulbs of the camas plant were harvested and prepared as a stable in their diet. This plant not only provided nourishment but also played a significant role in their cultural practices. Harvesting Practices The Kalapoya practiced sustainable harvesting methods for camas, ensuring that the plants could thrive for future generations. They used traditional techniques, such as baking the bulbs in underground ovens, to make them edible. This careful stewardship of the land and its resources reflects their deep understanding of the ecosystem. Cultural Significance Camas are considered a cultural keystone species, integral to the identity and traditions of the Kalapoya. The plant’s growth stages were often used as seasonal markers in their calendars, linking their agricultural practices to the rhythms of nature. The Kalapoya’s presence in the Beaverton area was driven by the availability of the camas and other resources, which were essential for their survival and cultural practice. They practice sustainable harvesting techniques, ensuring the plants thrived for future generations, and camas played a significant role in their cultural practices and seasonal ceremonies. They have lived in this area for over 14,000 years, developing a deep connection to the land and its resources. Importance of Camas Plants Camas plants were a vital food source for the Kalapoya. The bulbs of the camas plant were harvested and prepared as a stable in their diet. This plant not only provided nourishment but also played a significant role in their cultural practices. Harvesting Practices The Kalapoya practiced sustainable harvesting methods for camas, ensuring that the plants could thrive for future generations. They used traditional techniques, such as baking the bulbs in underground ovens, to make them edible. This careful stewardship of the land and its resources reflects their deep understanding of the ecosystem. Cultural Significance Camas are considered a cultural keystone species, integral to the identity and traditions of the Kalapoya. The plant’s growth stages were often used as seasonal markers in their calendars, linking their agricultural practices to the rhythms of nature. The Kalapoya’s presence in the Beaverton area was driven by the availability of the camas and other resources, which were essential for their survival and cultural practice. ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ Follow the Mural on Instagram: #gatewaytothepast On behalf of the Raleigh Hills Business Association, we want to thank the mural committee for all of their efforts in seeing this project from idea to completion. It took so many people to make this happen and we would be very remiss in not thanking you for all that you have pulled off. Jason - you had an idea, got the ball rolling, and this project would never have gotten off the ground without your tenacity and connections. Toni - for being the biggest behind the scenes player and making RHBA look fantastic in the process. You made this happen with your efforts at securing funding, contacting local dignitaries, and making sure we had a big audience and eyes on this project. We could never have pulled this off without you. Your belief in the value and community benefit of the mural would be hard to exceed. And now we have a coloring book too, something to help keep this in the community's eyes for some time to come. Gabrielle - you stepped in when the board needed some more eyes on the project. You made RHBA look good, competent, and above board, because of your efforts. We value your role and appreciate your offer to help out when it was needed. Diane - You retired from the board and stepped in with both feet to the mural project. Your positive disposition, phoning skills, talking it up with your connections definitely made a big impact. Over 60 people were counted at the celebration and so many RHBA members were there...maybe more than any one time previously. Bev - Never one to let a project linger, you stepped in to help out when it was needed. Your efforts have not gone unnoticed. We appreciate you for bringing so many people to the celebration, keeping the RHBA logo/brand/members out there front and center, and making sure we put a positive production together. Stacy Lynn Smith and Bob Hansen - for your artwork and creativity that brought a dream to fruition. Like most of RHBA events, it takes a village...some in the forefront, and others working hard behind the scenes. Thank you all for your part. Courtyard Village put together a great spread, and the room looked as lovely as ever. The weather was perfect. It was a great day to celebrate both indoors and outdoors. Fred Meyer allowed this project to go forward, Explore Tualatin Valley, Venture Portland helped to fund the mural. The thanks go on and all. We appreciate all the partners in helping show that RHBA is the Heart of the Community !

Kalapoya Natives and the Beaverton Area

The Kalapoya people originally occupied a vast area in the Willamette Valley. Including regions that encompass modern-day Beaverton, OR. They have lived in this area for over 14,000 years, developing a deep connection to the land and its resources.

They used traditional techniques, such as baking the bulbs in underground ovens, to make them edible. This careful stewardship of the land and its resources reflects their deep understanding of the ecosystem.

- You retired from the board and stepped in with both feet to the mural project. Your positive disposition, phoning skills, talking it up with your connections definitely made a big impact. Over 60 people were counted at the celebration and so many RHBA members were there...maybe more than any one time previously.

- Never one to let a project linger, you stepped in to help out when it was needed. Your efforts have not gone unnoticed. We appreciate you for bringing so many people to the celebration, keeping the RHBA logo/brand/members out there front and center, and making sure we put a positive production together.
Stacy Lynn Smith and Bob Hansen
- for your artwork and creativity that brought a dream to fruition.

Like most of RHBA events, it takes a village...some in the forefront, and others working hard behind the scenes. Thank you all for your part. Courtyard Village put together a great spread, and the room looked as lovely as ever. The weather was perfect. It was a great day to celebrate both indoors and outdoors. Fred Meyer allowed this project to go forward, Explore Tualatin Valley, Venture Portland helped to fund the mural. The thanks go on and all. We appreciate all the partners in helping show that
RHBA is the Heart of the Community

Nikki Wilson NJW Property Management 971-409-6735 njwpropertymgmt@frontier.com Nikki Wilson - NJW Property Management, LLC (Sept. 2016 Story) NJW Property Management LLC is a woman owned small business that has been in business since July 2012. MF Chase When you need web work you don’t need a geek or a friend…You need a Professional! http://www.mfchase.com 503 808-0021 Michael started his business in June 2008. Eco Binary…”thinking global and acting local” Business Expose' RHBA Founding Members Est 2010 (Story written Dec 2016) RHBA founding members, Linda Criss, RH Bank of the West, Branch Mgr; Toni Curry and Bev Snow-Kuehn, B-Line Enterprises came together to support the Raleigh Hills business community. The three ladies started talking, planning and envisioning what RHBA would look like; from a vision to reality, we officially launched in May of 2010. Building a local business community, shop local, know your business owners and your neighbors. Linda Criss was instrumental with all the legal matters, licenses, insurance, by-laws, the mission statement was a joint collaboration. Later the Board grew from three to four, adding Matt Chrisman, West Coast Bank RH Branch, Manager, then to five adding Diane Snedecor, Mountain Man Fruit & Nut Co. The two constants supporting RHBA are Michael Chase RHBA Webmaster, CF Chase Marketing, Robin Curtin RHBA Treasurer, Univera Associate and Joy Patterson former RHBA Secretary; PLUS the many RHBA business owners that continue to support the organization and we are now 76 members strong for the 2016-17 fiscal year. Toni Curry, with her new company, TLC Promotions, took the organization with a simple scrolly heart logo to a brand that is known and recognized. She used her graphic design background to the creation of a “real logo/a real brand” which included getting a grant from Venture Portland for boulevard banners which are proudly displaying the RHBA logo on B-H Hwy and streets in Raleigh Hills. Toni Curry and Linda Criss created position duties and put together an official RHBA policies and procedures manual. RHBA has had three Chairs in the six years since conception: Linda Criss, Bank of the West; Liz Utter, New Seasons Raleigh Hills and our current Chair Marie Morris, Beyond 18. Bev Snow-Kuehn and Diane Snedecor continue their outreach efforts and connecting the business community with our neighbors and encouraging new members to join RHBA. RHBA successes are: • Monthly business meeting at RH New Seasons • Sign toppers “Raleigh Hills Business District” • Member directory, December 2016 with 76 members • Boulevard Banners • Monthly presence in the Beaverton Resource Guide • Annual holiday party in December • Annual Shred / Recycle event during Earth Week in April Michael Wong of The Beaverton Resources Guide did a very nice write up for the December 2016 issue. Mary Sherman - Sherman Family Properties (February 2017 Story) The Sherman family has been part of the Raleigh Hills community for over 55 years. Our father, Bill Sherman, along with mother Dianne, began our business first as an insurance agency and later their interest turned to real estate. We are proud to carry on their tradition as Sherman Family Properties. We focus on local residential, retail and office properties with a few fun sunny get-aways in Palm Springs. Our passion is people, so we specialize in offering service and support to small business owners by caring for our tenants' experiences. We aim provide quality rental and leased space at a great value. Our family business also donates countless hours to better our community through volunteer service and leadership. Some of our flagship projects are The Raleigh Park Garden Tour for beautification of public spaces and to connect our neighbors with one another, and an annual project filling 300 holiday stockings to benefit the local Sunshine Pantry, which provides food, amenities, housewares, and clothing to people in need within our community. At home, our yard is a source for organic produce as well as a beautiful setting for garden tours and dinners for local non-profits. We like to think it also demonstrates how a small urban space can be an abundant food supply and how, by inviting our neighbors to use our wood oven and to visit the chickens, our garden can enrich our neighborhood. We also regularly host out of town guests interested in the urban farm, some through our Airbnb. RHBA, in the neighborhood where we work and live, brings us in contact with other businesses and strengthens the success of our community and our tenants. We are grateful for the long legacy we have had in Raleigh Hills and look forward to building strong ties with local small business well into the future. deanna-wilson Clients for Life: Every client is important to me, both as a client and a person. Client interests always come first! My goal is to do such a great job that you will call me every time you have a real estate need, and trust me enough to refer your friends, family and business associates to me. Personal: Born and raised in Oregon with seven brothers and sisters—a great start in developing my strong negotiating skills! My wonderful husband and I have a beautiful daughter and son. Outside of real estate, some of my favorite activities include hiking, bicycling, gardening and singing—recently on a hiking trip in the Lake District of England (the last train stop was at Windermere, which our company was named after). Professional Expertise: Principal Real Estate Broker helping buyers and sellers with their residential real estate needs for over 20 years. Designations include Certified Negotiation Expert (1% of Realtors nationwide), Certified Residential Specialist (4% of Realtors nationwide), Seniors Real Estate Specialist, Graduate Realtors Institute, and Certified Residential Investment Specialist. Practiced as a Certified Public Accountant in the Portland area for over 11 years. 1981 University of Oregon accounting graduate. “Selling a home can be difficult at the best of times, but Deanna made the whole process seem very easy. She was wonderful to work with, always very professional, very pleasant, and gave just the right advice to make things flow smoothly. I can’t imagine working with anyone else and highly recommend her to anyone who wants to buy or sell a house.” - G. Dakin, Seller “We described to Deanna the type of property we were looking for and without exception, each place she showed us met our specifications. Deanna was so knowledgeable and helpful and easy to talk to. At the end of a long day of looking at houses, Deanna suggested we go back to two of the places we were most interested in. That was a great idea, because it was at the second viewing that my husband and I truly fell in love with the place we ultimately made the offer on. We thought Deanna was helpful in the search process, but man did she shine when it came to the offer, inspection and negotiating for repairs part of the process. We felt like we truly had someone amazing in our corner, which we needed because we were pretty new to this. My husband and I had such a positive experience with Deanna and are incredibly thankful that we found her. We couldn’t recommend her more.” - M. Parton & D. Brown, Buyer

I can’t imagine working with anyone else and highly recommend her to anyone who wants to buy or sell a house.”

“We described to Deanna the type of property we were looking for and without exception, each place she showed us met our specifications. Deanna was so knowledgeable and helpful and easy to talk to. At the end of a long day of looking at houses, Deanna suggested we go back to two of the places we were most interested in. That was a great idea, because it was at the second viewing that my husband and I truly fell in love with the place we ultimately made the offer on. We thought Deanna was helpful in the search process, but man did she shine when it came to the offer, inspection and negotiating for repairs part of the process. We felt like we truly had someone amazing in our corner, which we needed because we were pretty new to this. My husband and I had such a positive experience with Deanna and are incredibly thankful that we found her. We couldn’t recommend her more.”

pages.textPlain: GHCL Annex Interior Garden Home Community Library 7475 SW Oleson Rd, Portland OR 97223 (located inside the THPRD Garden Home Recreation Center) Phone: 503-245-9932 Website: gardenhomelibrary.orgwccls.org Garden Home Community Library (GHCL) is a member of the Washington County Cooperative Library (WCCLS) system. The library is located inside the Tualatin Hills Parks & Recreation District’s Garden Home Recreation Center have been awarded the American Library Association “Star Library” rating for overall efficiency. More than just books Garden Home Community Library offers materials beyond books – for all ages. We also have movies, music, periodicals, video games, audiobooks, and e-books. We also have two Roku devices, a digital camera, a birding kit, Cultural Passes, and a Kill-a-Watt (electricity usage monitor). We get daily deliveries every day, so if it’s in Washington County library, you can have it sent here. GHCL ANNEX Garden Home Community Library ANNEX (formerly Garden Home Community Store) hosts library programming. The Annex is also now available as a meeting room for local not-for-profit groups during library hours. The ANNEX is equipped with WiFi, as well as a high definition projector with an 18-foot screen and surround sound. As this project advances, we will be adding additional equipment: one or more GoPro cameras, laptops for In-ANNEX use, a 3D printer, and all kinds of art supplies. See usage policy on our website: http://www.gardenhomelibrary.org/annex/. Please call or email gardenhomeannex@gmail.com if you have questions about using the GHCL ANNEX. Cultural Pass to Adventure The library has memberships to eight area attractions that can be reserved and checked out for a specific day. The Cultural Pass allows free entry for a specified number of people to the attraction. Available passes can be reserved online and you can print them from home. Ask in the library or visit https://www.wccls.org/culturalpass for more details. Programs for all ages Garden Home Community Library hosts free programs for all ages. Looking for something to do? For adults there is a monthly trivia night, a board game night, a table top gaming day, two book groups, film screenings, author visits, a writer’s group, and more. For children and families there are weekly story times (for babies, preschool and families), as well as craft events and monthly family movie days and other fun events. During the school year, reluctant readers can also sign up to read to Hannah, a 7-year-old golden retriever. (Reservations are required.) June events and programs: Visit our website – http://www.gardenhomelibrary.org/ – for more events or details All Programs are free and open to anyone. June 1 – Aug. 31 Summer Reading Programs for all ages Sign up to read all summer and keep those grey cells in shape. We’ll be kicking off our Summer Reading program on Weds., June 21 at the THPRD Community Night. Tues., June 6 at 6:30 pm “ Nerd Night: Trivia” for adults Location: Garden Home Community Library Annex (across the street, 7306 SW Oleson Rd, Portland 97223) Play solo or as a team. Prizes for those with most and least points at the end of the night. Weds., June 14 from 6-9 pm Board Game Night Location: Garden Home Community Library Annex (across the street, 7306 SW Oleson Rd, Portland 97223) Host Peter brings a new selection every month. You are invited to bring games you want to play too. Fri., June 16 at 7:30 pm (doors at 7) Friday Night Film Location: Garden Home Community Library Annex (across the street, 7306 SW Oleson Rd, Portland 97223) Film to be determined. Call library for details. Sun., June 25 from 1-5 Altera Vita Tabletop Roleplaying Location: Garden Home Community Library Annex (across the street, 7306 SW Oleson Rd, Portland 97223) Whether you’re feeling nostalgic or completely new to it all, join experienced game-master Eric for an afternoon of character sheets, fistfuls of dice, and collaborative storytelling in a different fictional world every month. If you’ve not visited Garden Home Community Library before, come check us out. And if you have, come say hello. Happy reading!

pages.textPlain: Neighborhood House http://nhpdx.org/ 7780 SW Capitol Hwy. Portland, OR 97219 ph.503.246.1663 501(c)(3) charitable tax exempt organization What We Do Across the Community and Across the Life-span Neighborhood House delivers innovative and life-changing programs for low-income people of all ages. Our highly-effective education and social support programs fall into the following core service areas: Early Childhood School-aged Youth Families in Need Seniors Our dedicated staff and volunteers work in schools, community centers and public housing sites, as well as our own facilities, to serve more than 18,000 people each year across the greater Portland area. Comprehensive Services Neighborhood House’s wide array of programs provide comprehensive services that effectively meet the needs of our low-income and senior clients, many who struggle with not just one, but multiple challenges. We believe our wrap-around service model is the most efficient and compassionate way to help people in need. Meeting the Needs of Diverse Communities Neighborhood House’s clients represent a remarkably diverse mix of ethnicities and cultures. To meet the needs of these diverse communities, Neighborhood House offers culturally specific services for African, Latino, Middle Eastern, and Russian immigrants in various programs and requires cultural competency of all programs and staff. 38% of our program staff are bicultural from cultures served by Neighborhood House; 12% more are bilingual. Neighborhood House staff regularly provide diversity training for other organizations, including Multnomah County, Portland Public Schools and Home Forward (formerly Housing Authority of Portland). Posted Dec. 8, 2016

pages.textPlain: Welcome! St. John is a place where a very diverse and active congregation enjoys the rich traditions of the Episcopal church. The church building is located on the Oregon Episcopal School campus in southwest Portland, Oregon, at 6300 SW Nicol Road. You can find us at the top of the hill, next to the bell tower. Our Mission As the Episcopal Parish of St. John the Baptist, we strive to share the Good News of God, to grow as Christian disciples, and to respond to the needs of the world in the power of the Holy Spirit. We do this by putting our faith into action through worship, education, reflection, and ministry to others. You can learn more about ministries and church activities by reading our Faith In Action blog, watching sermons on YouTube, or visiting our Facebook Page. Our Community We are an inclusive community where all are welcomed to come and explore their relationship with God. All baptized Christians (not just our members) are invited to join us in Holy Communion. While you are here, you will also be able relax in a peaceful environment, find joy in the music, listen to engaging sermons, and enjoy good fellowship with the others who have found their church home. Please call the Parish Office at (503) 245-3777 or sign the guest register so we can connect you with our Welcome Team. http://wheretraditionwelcomesdiversity.org/

pages.textPlain: Mission & Strategic Plan for West Slope Community Library Mission: West Slope Community Library provides people of all ages and backgrounds with access and guidance to information and materials that reflect all points of view. The Library serves residents of Washington County by being a resource and connecting people with materials and a space that meet their cultural, recreational, and lifelong learning needs. Strategic Plan: 2016-2020 Library Values: The West Slope Community Library serves the community by being a welcoming place that is inclusive, inviting, and informative with equal access for all. ·We embrace the diversity of the community. ·We are a place to learn, explore and imagine. ·We promote reading for education and pleasure. ·We offer a diverse collection of materials and programs. ·We value seizing new opportunities and adapting to new challenges. Goals 1. Welcoming Library Spaces: The library will serve people of all ages and backgrounds by being a vibrant neighborhood center that is attractive, safe, and welcoming to all. Activities: ·Improve customer service strategies through staff meetings, discussions and training. ·Petition Washington County to install walkways along 78th Ave to Raleigh Park and the library. ·Install bicycle parking. ·Hire a library space design consultant. ·Update exterior of library and install new exterior signs. ·Redesign front desk and public areas. ·Rearrange collection for users according to recommendations of the design consultant. 2. Information Technology and Instruction: Residents will have access to basic and emerging technology that connects them to the world of knowledge and information and with each other. Activities: ·Provide scanner and fax services. ·Provide color and wireless printing. ·Provide a basic technology manual for computer users. ·Develop app recommendation and discussion programs for adults, teens, and parents. ·Complete Edge Initiative library technology assessment. ·Help library staff meet technology competencies. (Developed by WCCLS Adult Services Committee.) 3. Reading Readiness: Infants, toddlers, preschool, elementary school children will have access to collections, programs, and services that will help them develop a lifelong love of books, reading, and learning. Activities: ·Provide Storytime’s for various ages and literacy levels. ·Provide summer reading programs. ·Develop programs for parents of preschoolers (e.g., sign language classes). ·Make connections with neighborhood preschools. ·Develop programs to inform parents about the library and library programs for infants, toddlers, and preschoolers. 4. Community Resources: Residents will have a central source for information about the wide variety of programs, services and activities provided by community agencies and organizations. Activities: ·Staff training on resources(211, WorkSource, schools) ·Develop and maintain an information packet for new community residents ·Compile reference documents for staff ·Promote resources though our website and social media. ·Build and organize a browsable collection of free community publications ·Present programs on Community Resources (earthquake preparedness, housing issue). ·Monitor local issues and respond with informational resources(e.g., NW earthquake alert) Maintain & Enhance Core Services: ·Reference and Readers Advisory : Residents will have someone to answer their questions on a wide array of topics of personal interest. ·Reading Readiness: Infants, toddlers and preschool children will have access to collections, programs, and services that will help them develop a lifelong love of books, reading and learning. ·New and Popular Material: Children, teens and adults will have access to materials, programs and services that simulate the imagination and provide a variety of leisure activities and experiences. ·Basic Technology Access: Residents will have access to basic and emerging technology that connects them to the world of knowledge and information. ·Lifelong Learning (programs): Residents will have the resources they need to explore topics of personal interest and continue to learn throughout their lives. West Slope Community Engagement: ·Raleigh Park Elementary School/PTO ·Raleigh Hills Elementary School ·Raleigh Hills Business Association ·Valley Presbyterian Church ·Tualatin Hills Park and Recreation ·Fir Grove Garden Club ·Local Preschools ·Local Businesses (Drinkery, Postal Annex, Courtyard Village, New Seasons, Starbucks, Advanced Camera) ·Eagle Scouts ·WorkSource Oregon ·Washington County Sheriff (national night out) ·Local Artists Acknowledgements: Facilitator: Linda Lybecker Planning Committee ·Doug Bigelow / Advisory Board ·Lee Ann Barth / Advisory Board ·Alice Murphy / Advisory Board ·Jane Weissman / Advisory Board ·Kirsten Webb / Friends of West Slope Library WCCLS Staff ·Veronica Eden ·PJ Bentley ·Kirsten Freeman-Benson Posted March 29, 2017

blocks.text: Mission & Strategic Plan for West Slope Community Library

Mission: West Slope Community Library provides people of all ages and backgrounds with access and guidance to information and materials that reflect all points of view. The Library serves residents of Washington County by being a resource and connecting people with materials and a space that meet their cultural, recreational, and lifelong learning needs.
Library Values: The West Slope Community Library serves the community by being a welcoming place that is inclusive, inviting, and informative with equal access for all.
·We embrace the diversity of the community.
·We are a place to learn, explore and imagine.
·We promote reading for education and pleasure.
·We offer a diverse collection of materials and programs.
·We value seizing new opportunities and adapting to new challenges.
1. Welcoming Library Spaces: The library will serve people of all ages and backgrounds by being a vibrant neighborhood center that is attractive, safe, and welcoming to all.
·Improve customer service strategies through staff meetings, discussions and training.
·Petition Washington County to install walkways along 78th Ave to Raleigh Park and the library.
·Update exterior of library and install new exterior signs.
·Rearrange collection for users according to recommendations of the design consultant.
2. Information Technology and Instruction: Residents will have access to basic and emerging technology that connects them to the world of knowledge and information and with each other.
·Provide a basic technology manual for computer users.
·Develop app recommendation and discussion programs for adults, teens, and parents.
·Help library staff meet technology competencies. (Developed by WCCLS Adult Services Committee.)

3. Reading Readiness: Infants, toddlers, preschool, elementary school children will have access to collections, programs, and services that will help them develop a lifelong love of books, reading, and learning.
·Provide Storytime’s for various ages and literacy levels.
·Develop programs for parents of preschoolers (e.g., sign language classes).
·Develop programs to inform parents about the library and library programs for infants, toddlers, and preschoolers.
4. Community Resources: Residents will have a central source for information about the wide variety of programs, services and activities provided by community agencies and organizations.
·Develop and maintain an information packet for new community residents
·Promote resources though our website and social media.
·Build and organize a browsable collection of free community publications
·Present programs on Community Resources (earthquake preparedness, housing issue).
·Monitor local issues and respond with informational resources(e.g., NW earthquake alert)

·New and Popular Material: Children, teens and adults will have access to materials, programs and services that simulate the imagination and provide a variety of leisure activities and experiences.
·Basic Technology Access: Residents will have access to basic and emerging technology that connects them to the world of knowledge and information.

pages.description: Westside Economic Alliance Mission is to: Create an environment conducive to business growth, Serve as a unified voice for members on public policy issues affecting the Westside of the Portland metropolitan region, Promote and improve the visibility of the Westside as a premier economic development locale for businesses to locate, expand, and thrive Challenge and
pages.textPlain: Westside Economic Alliance Mission is to: Create an environment conducive to business growth, Serve as a unified voice for members on public policy issues affecting the Westside of the Portland metropolitan region, Promote and improve the visibility of the Westside as a premier economic development locale for businesses to locate, expand, and thrive Challenge and influence policy decision and regulations impacting the economic vitality of our communities. Geographically, our members are located or do business mostly in Washington County and/or western Clackamas County. All of the major cities in Washington County are members (Wilsonville, Tualatin, Tigard, Sherwood, Beaverton, Hillsboro, Cornelius, and Forest Grove), along with the county itself and the special service districts too. A few of the school districts are also members. On the private side, we tend to have larger employers as members (Intel, Nike, Genentech, Legacy Health, Providence Health & Services, Tuality Healthcare, Fred Meyer, and etc.) and the commercial real estate investors. We often host events (mainly Breakfast Forums) exploring topics that are timely and interesting to the business community. Our most popular annual event is the “State of Washington County,” which typically happens in March. The date for 2017 hasn’t been set yet. Our Breakfast Forums are usually held the 4th Thursday of the month (10 months out of the year) at 7:30 a.m. at the Embassy Suites in Tigard, but we do change dates and locations from time to time due to speaker or venue availability. In July, we don’t have a Breakfast Forum because it’s when we host our annual fundraiser – the Westside Golf Scramble. We also don’t host one in November, since the 4th Thursday is Thanksgiving, so we usually combine November and December, hosting one at the beginning of December. Our executive director has a monthly column in Pamplin Media’s Business Tribune. It is published the 2nd week of the month. We also produce a weekly email newsletter. Westside Economic Alliance 503-968-3100 10220 SW Nimbus Ave., Ste. K-12 Tigard, Oregon 97223 http://westsidealliance.org/ Posted February 28, 2017

blocks.text: Westside Economic Alliance Mission is to:

items: Create an environment conducive to business growth,
Serve as a unified voice for members on public policy issues affecting the Westside of the Portland metropolitan region,
Promote and improve the visibility of the Westside as a premier economic development locale for businesses to locate, expand, and thrive
Challenge and influence policy decision and regulations impacting the economic vitality of our communities.

blocks.text: Geographically, our members are located or do business mostly in Washington County and/or western Clackamas County. All of the major cities in Washington County are members (Wilsonville, Tualatin, Tigard, Sherwood, Beaverton, Hillsboro, Cornelius, and Forest Grove), along with the county itself and the special service districts too. A few of the school districts are also members. On the private side, we tend to have larger employers as members (Intel, Nike, Genentech, Legacy Health, Providence Health & Services, Tuality Healthcare, Fred Meyer, and etc.) and the commercial real estate investors.

We often host events (mainly Breakfast Forums) exploring topics that are timely and interesting to the business community. Our most popular annual event is the “State of Washington County,” which typically happens in March. The date for 2017 hasn’t been set yet. Our Breakfast Forums are usually held the 4th Thursday of the month (10 months out of the year) at 7:30 a.m. at the Embassy Suites in Tigard, but we do change dates and locations from time to time due to speaker or venue availability.

In July, we don’t have a Breakfast Forum because it’s when we host our annual fundraiser – the Westside Golf Scramble. We also don’t host one in November, since the 4th Thursday is Thanksgiving, so we usually combine November and December, hosting one at the beginning of December.
Our executive director has a monthly column in Pamplin Media’s Business Tribune. It is published the 2nd week of the month. We also produce a weekly email newsletter.

Your donations, support and volunteerism is an important facet of the services we provide to our local community.RHBA matches donations made by our members and friends up to $750.00 for to a collective amount of $1500.00 Our RHBA member Amy Swallow is taking the lead on this years SWAC project email amy@stuffadjust.com or callhttps://www.stuffadjust.com 503-740-2406To donate, volunteer or learn more about the SWAC Project visit the link below or email us at: info@raleighhillsbusinessassn.orgDonations/Fund Raising OptionsYou can also send a check to: RHBA 10445 SW Canyon Rd, Suite 116, Beaverton OR 97005Or bring cash or check to the RHBA Coffee on Nov 12th or to Lunch on Nov 19th.Jump on Board for the SWAC Guessing Game, created by Amy Swallow!!!How does a container of candy help SWAC? Annual Shop With A Cop was another successful event. RHBA partners with Washington County Sheriff’s Foundation every December. Providing 15 students (K-5) from Raleigh Park Elementary School. Giving the students the opportunity to partner with Washington County Deputies for shopping trip helping make their families’ holiday a little brighter. Contact RHBA for more info: info@RaleighHillsBusinessAssn.org This is our biggest social action project of the year. For more than ten years, RHBA has supported a cooperative project with Beaverton School District and Washington County Sheriff’s Office. This project creates a holiday experience for 15 Raleigh Park Elementary School families. RHBA matches donations made by RHBA members and friends up to $750 for a collective amount of $1,500. The donations help purchase gifts for all members of the 15 families. Other members donate by providing lunch for the kids with the deputies, craft items to make during lunch, gift wrapping, and shopping for the selected students. It is a great big project with many moving parts that offers lots of opportunity to participate. We have members who were Shop with a Cop recipients when they kids and speak about how much the program meant to them and their families. So what can you do? Make a donation. You can do it online at the RHBA website. Scroll down and on the right hand side look for a blue lettered Shop with a Cop and that will lead you to the donation page. All donations needed! If shopping is your thing, the pre-shopping event without deputies and kids but with WCSO staff, is happening on a selected date in early December. Meet at Fred Meyer on 11425 SW Beaverton-Hillsdale Hwy. Six volunteers needed. Like to gift wrap…there’s a big wrapping party on following selected date in December 3rd. Seven Volunteers needed. Shopping with kids and deputies at Beaverton Fred Meyer at 11:30am. Followed by lunch at Raleigh Park School at 12:30. Five volunteers needed to set up lunch at school. Reply to info@RaleighHillsBusinessAssn.org ! Times confirmed to all interested parties. ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Washington County Sheriff's Office Each year, the Washington County Sheriff’s Office engages with local families through their annual “Shop With a Cop” holiday event. The Sheriff’s Office Foundation supports funding for this special program, spreading holiday magic and cheer with many deserving families in our community. The Washington County Sheriff’s Office coordinates with local elementary schools to nominate families who could benefit from this program. Students pair with deputies to shop for their families, promoting positive and meaningful interactions with law enforcement as they work together to fulfill the families’ wish and needs lists. The donations and support from the Sheriff’s Office Foundation, along with local businesses and organizations, make these special Shop With a Cop events possible. If you would like to help the Shop with a Cop program in its mission of aiding local families, please consider a donation to the Sheriff's Office Foundation. double heart logo
